adapty.pricing_summary: Free until $5,000 monthly revenue, then 1% of monthly revenue; Enterprise is custom, with revenue-based add-ons priced separately.
pros: Bundles purchase handling, remote paywalls, and experimentation under one SDK, so you do not stitch together separate tools to test monetization.
The Pro plan stays free until $5,000 in monthly revenue and then charges 1% of revenue, so cost tracks what the app earns (checked July 19, 2026).
SDKs are open source and cover iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, and web, which helps if the app is or becomes cross-platform.
Web payments through Stripe and Paddle let you sell outside the App Store when that fits your funnel.
cons: Entitlement and paywall logic run through the SDK, which is a dependency you would have to unwind to migrate away later.
Revenue-based add-ons such as UA Tools and Apple Ads Manager are priced on top of the Pro rate, so total cost can climb past the headline 1%.
The experimentation and analytics surface is a lot to learn if you only ever wanted to turn purchases on.
You still create and maintain products in App Store Connect yourself, so Adapty does not remove that store setup.
adapty.body_markdown:
Adapty is subscription infrastructure paired with a no-code paywall builder, A/B testing, and revenue analytics for mobile apps. It handles receipt validation, entitlements, and cross-platform purchase state through its SDKs, then adds a visual paywall editor and an experimentation layer on top so you can change and test paywalls without shipping a new build. For a solo iOS developer, the draw is the combination: reliable purchase handling plus tools aimed squarely at moving conversion and lifetime value.

It fits best once you are ready to iterate on monetization rather than just turn purchases on. If you only need a thin receipt validator and never plan to test paywalls, the analytics and experimentation surface is more than the app requires. It overlaps heavily with [RevenueCat](/tools/revenuecat), so the choice often comes down to which paywall and experimentation workflow you prefer.

## Pricing
Adapty charges a single Pro rate that stays free under a revenue threshold, then scales as a percentage of revenue, with paid add-ons for extra tools.

:::comparison Adapty plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Pro | Free until $5,000 monthly revenue, then 1% of monthly revenue | Open-source SDKs, web payments, cross-platform sync, revenue analytics, the paywall builder, A/B testing, and email support |
| Enterprise | Custom | Everything in Pro plus onboarding and migration, a dedicated success manager, custom SLAs, data residency, and SOC 2 Type II |
:::

Adapty also sells revenue-based add-ons such as Refund Saver, UA Tools, and Apple Ads Manager, each free under $5,000 monthly revenue and then priced separately. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Adapty provides open-source SDKs for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, and web, plus a REST API and server-side webhooks. On iOS you add the SDK through Swift Package Manager, create your products and subscription groups in App Store Connect, and map them to what Adapty calls access levels in the dashboard. The paywall builder then renders paywalls you can update remotely, and the SDK reports subscription state you check in code.

Expect the usual subscription friction rather than a shortcut around it. You still configure products in App Store Connect and test purchases in the sandbox on a real device, which is the slow part. Because paywalls and experiments are configured remotely, decide on a naming scheme for placements and access levels early, or the dashboard gets hard to reason about.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Adapty free for a small app?
Yes, up to a point. The Pro plan is free until $5,000 in monthly revenue, then costs 1% of monthly revenue (checked July 19, 2026). A pre-revenue or low-revenue app can run on it without paying.

### How is Adapty different from RevenueCat?
Both are subscription infrastructure with paywalls and analytics. [RevenueCat](/tools/revenuecat) is broad purchase infrastructure with a large integration catalog, while Adapty centers its visual paywall builder and built-in A/B testing. Pricing is close, since both are free under a revenue threshold and then charge a percentage of revenue.

### Does Adapty replace App Store Connect?
No. You still create products, prices, and subscription groups in App Store Connect and submit the purchase flow for review. Adapty sits on top, handling validation, entitlements, and the paywall you present.

superwall.pricing_summary: Subscription infrastructure is free at any scale; paywalls are free up to $10,000 monthly paywall-attributed revenue, then 1% of that attributed revenue.
pros: Billing is tied to paywall-attributed revenue, so you pay only on subscriptions the paywalls actually converted rather than on total app revenue (checked July 19, 2026).
The subscription infrastructure layer, including entitlements and receipt validation, is free at any scale, so you can adopt that part without a revenue-based fee.
Registering placements in code instead of hardcoding paywall views keeps monetization changes on the dashboard side, so you can iterate without an app release.
It can run alongside an existing purchase backend, so you do not have to rip out what you already have to start testing paywalls.
cons: Moving paywall behavior into remote config means an outage or misconfiguration there affects what users see, so you need a sensible fallback.
It is a focused paywall tool, not a general backend, so data storage, auth, and non-purchase features stay your responsibility.
Attribution-based pricing can be harder to forecast than a flat fee, since your cost depends on how much revenue the paywalls convert.
The value shows up mainly once you are actively running paywall experiments, so before that the infrastructure alone overlaps with free options.
superwall.body_markdown:
Superwall is a paywall platform for subscription apps. Its focus is the paywall itself: you design native paywalls in a remote editor, target and schedule them by audience, and run experiments to test copy, layout, and pricing without shipping a new build. It also offers a free subscription infrastructure layer, so entitlements, purchase APIs, receipt validation, webhooks, and analytics are available alongside the paywall product.

It fits best when paywall iteration is the thing you want to move and you are comfortable letting a remote tool drive what users see. If you have not launched paywalls yet, or you want a single vendor to own your whole backend and data model, Superwall is narrower than that by design. Teams often weigh it against [RevenueCat](/tools/revenuecat), which leads with broad purchase infrastructure rather than the paywall as the product.

## Pricing
Superwall keeps its subscription infrastructure free at any scale and charges only on paywall-attributed revenue above a threshold.

:::comparison Superwall plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Infrastructure | Free at any scale | Entitlements, purchase APIs, receipt validation, webhooks, revenue analytics, and SQL access through the Query API |
| Paywalls | Free up to $10,000 monthly paywall-attributed revenue, then 1% of paywall-attributed revenue | The remote paywall editor, audience targeting, and A/B experiments |
:::

You are billed only on revenue that Superwall paywalls actually converted, so subscriptions it did not help earn are never charged. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Superwall provides SDKs for iOS, Android, React Native, and Flutter. On iOS you add the SDK, then present paywalls by registering placements in code rather than hardcoding a paywall view; the paywall itself is configured and updated from the dashboard. If you use the free infrastructure layer, the SDK also handles entitlements and purchase state, so you can run Superwall on its own or alongside another purchase backend.

Plan for the remote-first model. Because paywalls live in the dashboard, a lot of monetization behavior moves out of the codebase, which is convenient but means placement names and audience rules need discipline. You still define products in App Store Connect and test purchases in the sandbox on a device, and paywalls that depend on remote config want a sensible fallback if that config is unavailable.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Superwall free to use?
The subscription infrastructure layer is free at any scale, and the paywall product is free up to $10,000 in monthly paywall-attributed revenue, then 1% of that attributed revenue (checked July 19, 2026). Revenue from subscriptions its paywalls did not convert is not billed.

### How is Superwall different from Adapty?
Both offer remote paywalls and experiments. [Adapty](/tools/adapty) presents itself as full subscription infrastructure with analytics and a paywall builder, while Superwall centers the paywall and charges strictly on paywall-attributed revenue rather than on total revenue, with its infrastructure layer kept free.

### Do I still need App Store Connect and product setup?
Yes. Superwall controls how and when paywalls appear, but you create the products, prices, and subscription groups in App Store Connect and submit them for review yourself.

qonversion.pricing_summary: Free up to $10,000 monthly tracked revenue, then Starter at 0.6% and Growth at 0.8% of revenue; Enterprise is custom.
pros: The free tier reaches $10,000 in monthly tracked revenue and includes unlimited apps and seats, which is generous if you ship several small apps (checked July 19, 2026).
Paid plans are 0.6% to 0.8% of revenue, lower headline rates than the 1% common elsewhere in the category (checked July 19, 2026).
It bundles a customer CRM view alongside the SDK, so you can inspect individual subscriber state without building your own reporting.
SDKs span iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, and web, so the same purchase layer carries across platforms.
cons: Key features such as A/B experiments and raw data export are gated to the Growth plan, so the low entry rate does not include everything.
Entitlement logic runs through the SDK, which is a dependency you would need to replace to leave the platform.
It is a smaller platform than some competitors, so third-party integrations and community examples are fewer.
You still set up and maintain products in App Store Connect, so Qonversion does not remove that store configuration.
qonversion.body_markdown:
Qonversion is subscription infrastructure with revenue analytics, a customer data layer, no-code paywalls, and experiments for mobile apps. Its SDKs handle purchases and entitlements across platforms, and the dashboard adds analytics, a lightweight CRM view of customers, and paywall tooling. For a solo iOS developer, it is a middle-ground option: purchase handling plus data and paywall features without committing to one of the larger platforms in the category.

It fits apps that already sell subscriptions and want reporting and experiments in the same place as the SDK. If your app has no purchase flow, none of this applies. If you are comparing platforms closely, you will find it lines up feature-for-feature with [RevenueCat](/tools/revenuecat) and [Adapty](/tools/adapty), so the decision often comes down to pricing and which dashboard you prefer.

## Pricing
Qonversion has a free tier under a tracked-revenue threshold, then 2 percentage-of-revenue plans and a custom enterprise plan.

:::comparison Qonversion plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | Free up to $10,000 monthly tracked revenue | Subscription SDKs, basic analytics, the customer CRM, the no-code paywall builder, Stripe and Paddle, and unlimited apps and seats |
| Starter | 0.6% of revenue | Everything in Free plus advanced analytics, basic integrations, and webhooks |
| Growth | 0.8% of revenue | Everything in Starter plus Apple Search Ads, A/B experiments, refund handling, raw data export, and priority support |
| Enterprise | Custom | Everything in Growth plus custom pricing, a dedicated success manager, and priority technical support |
:::

The percentage plans are billed on tracked revenue, and the free tier includes unlimited apps and seats up to the threshold. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Qonversion provides SDKs for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, and web, plus a REST API and webhooks. On iOS you add the SDK, define your products and subscription groups in App Store Connect, and map them to entitlements and offerings in the Qonversion dashboard. The SDK then reports entitlement state you check in code, and paywalls can be configured without a new release.

Expect standard subscription setup work. You still create products in App Store Connect and run sandbox purchases on a real device to verify the flow. One planning note: several capabilities you may assume are included, such as A/B experiments and raw data export, sit on the Growth plan rather than the free tier, so map the features you need to a plan before building around them.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Qonversion free for a small app?
Yes. The Free plan covers apps up to $10,000 in monthly tracked revenue and includes the SDKs, basic analytics, and the no-code paywall builder, with unlimited apps and seats (checked July 19, 2026). Above that threshold you move to a percentage-of-revenue plan.

### How is Qonversion different from RevenueCat?
Both are subscription infrastructure with analytics and paywalls. [RevenueCat](/tools/revenuecat) is free up to $2,500 monthly tracked revenue and then 1% of tracked revenue, while Qonversion is free up to $10,000 tracked revenue and then 0.6% to 0.8% depending on plan (checked July 19, 2026), so the pricing shapes differ even where the features overlap.

### Which plan includes A/B experiments?
Experiments are on the Growth plan, which is 0.8% of revenue, rather than the free or Starter tiers (checked July 19, 2026). If paywall testing is central to your plan, budget for that tier.

firebase.pricing_summary: The Spark plan is free within generous quotas; the Blaze plan adds metered pay-as-you-go pricing through Google Cloud, while analytics and Crashlytics stay free on both.
pros: The always-free tier bundles Analytics, Crashlytics, Cloud Messaging, and Remote Config, so you get production-grade monitoring and push without adding a paid service.
A single SDK covers auth, database, storage, and functions, which cuts the number of vendors a solo developer has to wire together and maintain.
Cloud Firestore syncs data across devices in near real time and works offline, so shared or multi-device app state is handled for you.
The Spark plan lets you build and launch without a payment method, and Blaze only starts charging once you pass the free quotas.
cons: Blaze bills per unit of usage through Google Cloud, so an inefficient query or a traffic spike can produce a surprising bill without budget alerts in place.
Firestore security rules are a separate language you must learn and test, and one permissive rule can expose your whole database.
Migrating away is hard because your data lives in a proprietary document model and your app depends on Google's SDK conventions.
Ad-hoc reporting and complex queries are awkward on a document store, so heavy analysis often means exporting data to BigQuery.
firebase.body_markdown:
Firebase is Google's app development platform. It bundles authentication, two NoSQL databases (Cloud Firestore and the Realtime Database), file storage, serverless Cloud Functions, hosting, and a large free monitoring layer (Analytics, Crashlytics, Cloud Messaging, and Remote Config) behind one SDK. For a solo iOS developer, the draw is breadth: add sign-in, sync data across devices, send push notifications, and watch crashes land in Crashlytics without running a server.

Where it fits less well: Firestore and the Realtime Database are document stores, not relational tables, so if your data is highly relational and you want SQL, joins, and constraints, a Postgres-backed option like [Supabase](/tools/supabase) is a better fit. Firebase also ties you to Google Cloud billing and conventions, which some builders prefer to avoid.

## Pricing
Firebase has two plans: the no-cost Spark plan and the pay-as-you-go Blaze plan.

:::comparison Firebase plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Spark | $0 per month | Free quotas including 50,000 Authentication monthly active users, 1 GiB Firestore storage, 50,000 reads and 20,000 writes per day, 2 million Cloud Functions calls per month, and 10 GB hosting |
| Blaze | Pay-as-you-go on top of the free quotas | Everything in Spark plus metered usage past the free tier, for example $0.40 per million Cloud Functions calls; eligible accounts get $300 in credit |
:::

Analytics, Crashlytics, Cloud Messaging, App Distribution, Performance Monitoring, and Remote Config stay free on both plans, and Blaze bills through Google Cloud so costs track real usage. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Firebase provides a native iOS SDK you add with Swift Package Manager, plus SDKs for Android, Web, Flutter, and Unity. You create a project in the Firebase console, download the GoogleService-Info.plist file into your Xcode target, and import only the modules you use, such as Auth, Firestore, or Crashlytics. Expect an afternoon to get sign-in and a first database read working.

Two gotchas are worth planning for. Firestore security rules are their own language and are easy to leave too open, so budget time to write and test them before launch. And because Blaze is metered, a runaway query or a misconfigured client can drive real cost, so set budget alerts in Google Cloud early.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Firebase free for a small app?
Yes, within the Spark quotas. A small iOS app can run on no-cost limits such as 50,000 authentication monthly active users and 50,000 Firestore reads per day (checked July 19, 2026), moving to Blaze only when you exceed them or need services Spark does not cover.

### How is Firebase different from Supabase?
Both give you auth, a database, storage, and serverless functions behind an SDK. The core difference is the database: Firebase uses NoSQL document stores, while [Supabase](/tools/supabase) is built on Postgres with SQL, relations, and row-level security. Firebase leans realtime and document-first; Supabase leans relational.

### Does Firebase cover analytics and crash reporting?
Yes. Google Analytics for Firebase, Crashlytics, and Performance Monitoring are free on both plans, which is why many iOS builders adopt Firebase for crash reporting even when their backend lives elsewhere.

appwrite.pricing_summary: A free cloud tier, a Pro plan from $25 per month with metered overages, and custom Enterprise pricing; the open-source server is free to self-host.
pros: The Pro plan is a flat $25 per month rather than metered per-service billing, so costs stay predictable as usage grows within the plan limits.
A single Apple SDK supports iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS, which helps if you ship the same account and data model across Apple platforms.
Because the same API runs in the cloud and on a self-hosted server, you can start on the managed free tier and move to your own infrastructure later without rewriting client code.
Features arrive as an integrated set of databases, functions, storage, and messaging, so you configure one platform instead of stitching several services together.
cons: Appwrite Cloud is younger than the largest backend platforms, so some third-party integrations and long-tail documentation are still filling in.
Free-tier projects pause after a week of inactivity, which can surprise you on a slow side project or during a quiet development stretch.
Self-hosting shifts uptime, security patches, backups, and scaling onto you, so the free-to-run server still costs real time.
The platform surface is broad, so configuring auth methods, permissions, and function runtimes correctly takes some ramp-up.
appwrite.body_markdown:
Appwrite is an open-source backend platform. It gives you authentication, databases, file storage, serverless functions, and messaging behind a REST and GraphQL API, and it ships as either a managed cloud service or a self-hosted deployment you run with Docker. For a solo iOS developer, the appeal is a familiar backend feature set you can start on the free cloud tier and later move onto your own servers without changing vendors.

Where it fits less well: Appwrite's database is its own abstraction rather than raw SQL, so if you want to write Postgres queries directly, [Supabase](/tools/supabase) is closer to that model. Appwrite Cloud is also younger than [Firebase](/tools/firebase), so some managed-service polish and integrations are still catching up.

## Pricing
Appwrite Cloud has a free tier, a flat Pro plan, and custom Enterprise pricing; the open-source server is free to self-host.

:::comparison Appwrite Cloud plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | $0 per month | 75,000 monthly active users, 5 GB bandwidth, 2 GB storage, 750,000 function executions, and up to 2 projects that pause after a week of inactivity |
| Pro | From $25 per month | 200,000 monthly active users, 2 TB bandwidth, 150 GB storage, 3.5 million executions, unlimited projects, daily backups, and email support, with overages such as $15 per 100 GB of extra bandwidth |
| Enterprise | Custom | SLAs, dedicated support, SOC 2 and HIPAA compliance, and custom infrastructure |
:::

Self-hosting the open-source server carries no license fee; you pay only for the servers you run it on. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Appwrite provides an Apple SDK for Swift covering iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS, alongside SDKs for Android, Flutter, React Native, and Web. On iOS you add the SDK through Swift Package Manager, point a Client at your project endpoint (cloud or self-hosted), and then call the Account, Databases, and Storage services. Getting email or OAuth sign-in working is a short exercise.

Two things to plan for. OAuth callbacks on iOS need a registered URL scheme, which is a common first-time snag. And if you self-host, you take on running and updating the Docker stack, backups, and scaling yourself, which is real work the cloud tier removes.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Appwrite really free?
In two ways. The open-source server is free to self-host, so you pay only for hosting. Appwrite Cloud also has a free tier with 75,000 monthly active users, 5 GB bandwidth, and 750,000 function executions (checked July 19, 2026), though free projects pause after a week of inactivity.

### How is Appwrite different from Firebase?
Both bundle auth, a database, storage, and functions behind an SDK. The main differences are openness and hosting: Appwrite is open source and can be self-hosted, while [Firebase](/tools/firebase) is a proprietary Google service tied to Google Cloud billing. If avoiding vendor lock-in or owning your infrastructure matters, Appwrite gives you that path.

### Can I self-host to avoid the cloud fees?
Yes. The server is open source and runs with Docker, so you can host it yourself and skip the cloud plans. The trade-off is that you own uptime, updates, backups, and scaling, which the Pro plan handles for you.

telemetrydeck.pricing_summary: A free tier of 50,000 signals per month for new accounts (100,000 for older accounts), then paid plans from 9 euros per month priced by monthly signal volume.
pros: Because it hashes identifiers and avoids personal data, adopting it keeps your App Store privacy label simple and usually removes the need for a consent prompt.
The Swift SDK is small and covers iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS, so one integration serves your whole Apple lineup.
Pricing is tied to signal volume rather than per-seat licensing, so the cost depends on how much your app sends, not on how many people view the dashboards.
The free tier is enough to run real analytics for a small app, so you can measure from day one at no cost.
cons: Signals are aggregated and identifiers are hashed, so you cannot inspect an individual user's full journey the way an identity-based analytics tool allows.
The paid tiers scale with signal volume, so a high-traffic or event-heavy app can climb tiers quickly if you are not selective about what you send.
The July 2026 pricing change lowered the free allowance for new accounts to 50,000 signals per month, so the starting headroom is smaller than it once was.
The feature set is intentionally narrow, so session replay, feature flags, and experiments are simply not part of the product.
telemetrydeck.body_markdown:
TelemetryDeck is privacy-first product analytics aimed at Apple platform developers. You send signals (events) from your app, and it shows dashboards for active users, retention, adoption, and custom funnels without collecting personal identifiers or tracking users across apps. For a solo iOS developer, the pitch is analytics that respect privacy by design and keep App Store privacy labels simple.

Where it fits less well: TelemetryDeck is deliberately lightweight and privacy-preserving, so if you need ad attribution, per-user journeys, or marketing-oriented growth tooling, a heavier platform like [PostHog](/tools/posthog) or [Amplitude](/tools/amplitude) will do more. It is a focused analytics tool, not a full product-experimentation suite.

## Pricing
TelemetryDeck has a free tier plus paid plans priced by the volume of signals you send each month.

:::comparison TelemetryDeck plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | 0 euros per month | 50,000 signals per month for accounts created after July 1, 2026 (accounts created earlier keep 100,000), and full analytics dashboards |
| Paid | From 9 euros per month | Higher monthly signal volumes in granular tiers priced by signal count, billed monthly or annually |
:::

Paid tiers step up by signal volume rather than by feature, and annual billing is discounted; exact per-tier prices are shown in the pricing calculator. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
TelemetryDeck provides a native Swift SDK for iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS, added through Swift Package Manager, plus options for Android and Web. You create an app in the dashboard to get an app ID, initialize the SDK at launch, and call a send function wherever you want to record a signal. Basic analytics can be live in under an hour.

Two things to know. TelemetryDeck hashes identifiers on the client and does not receive raw device or user IDs, which keeps it privacy-preserving but also means you cannot reconstruct a single named user's journey. And because you bill by signal volume, it is worth being deliberate about which events you send so a chatty app does not burn through the free tier.

## Frequently asked questions
### What is a signal, and how many will I use?
A signal is one event sent from your app, such as a screen view or an action. TelemetryDeck suggests estimating with the number of users multiplied by 30 as a starting point (checked July 19, 2026). The free tier covers 50,000 signals per month for new accounts.

### How is TelemetryDeck different from PostHog?
Both give you product analytics, but the philosophies differ. TelemetryDeck is privacy-first and deliberately minimal, hashing identifiers so no personal data is stored. [PostHog](/tools/posthog) is a broader platform with session replay, feature flags, and experiments, which is more capability at the cost of more setup and a larger data footprint. Choose TelemetryDeck for privacy and simplicity; choose PostHog for breadth.

### Does using TelemetryDeck affect my App Store privacy label?
Because it is designed not to collect personal identifiers or track users across apps, TelemetryDeck is straightforward to declare on an App Store privacy label and does not require a consent prompt for basic analytics. You still declare exactly what your app sends, so review your own signal payloads.

amplitude.pricing_summary: Free for up to 2 million events per month with unlimited seats; the Plus plan starts at $0 and scales with event volume, and Growth and Enterprise are custom.
pros: The free plan allows up to 2 million events per month with unlimited seats, so a small app can run real product analytics without paying or rationing logins (checked July 19, 2026).
Experiments, guides, and surveys live in the same platform as analytics, so you can act on a finding without adopting a second tool.
SDKs cover iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, web, and a server API, which keeps event definitions consistent if you ship on more than one surface.
Behavioral cohorts and retention analysis go deeper than simple dashboards, which helps once you are optimizing activation rather than counting installs.
cons: The depth only pays off with a disciplined event taxonomy; a messy naming scheme makes reports harder to trust, not easier.
Verbose autocapture can burn through the free event allowance quickly and push you onto usage-based paid pricing sooner than expected.
Paid Growth and Enterprise pricing is custom and quote-based, so the cost at scale is not something you can read off the page in advance.
It collects detailed behavioral data, which adds privacy and App Store data-disclosure obligations you would not have with an aggregate-only tool.
amplitude.body_markdown:
Amplitude is a product analytics platform for understanding how people move through your app: funnels, retention, cohorts, and behavioral analysis, with experiments, guides, and surveys layered on top. For a solo iOS developer, it answers the questions that matter after launch, such as where users drop off in onboarding and which features drive retention, without you building a reporting pipeline.

It fits once you have enough traffic that behavioral questions are worth asking and you expect analytics to be an ongoing workflow rather than a launch checkbox. If you only need a few aggregate counters or a privacy-light view of activation, a lighter option such as [TelemetryDeck](/tools/telemetrydeck) is less to learn and less to maintain. Amplitude rewards a clean event taxonomy, and without one its depth turns into noise.

## Pricing
Amplitude uses a freemium model with a large free tier and usage-based paid plans priced on event volume.

:::comparison Amplitude plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | $0 forever, up to 2 million events per month | Core product analytics, unlimited seats, 10,000 monthly session replays, and limited experiments, guides, and surveys |
| Plus | Starts at $0 and scales with event volume, up to 70 million events | Everything in Free plus custom events and formulas, 20 behavioral cohorts, alerts, and 2-year data retention |
| Growth | Custom, based on event volume | Everything in Plus plus advanced behavioral analysis, monitoring and alerts, SSO, and project permissions |
| Enterprise | Custom | Everything in Growth plus unlimited projects, data access controls, and role-based access control |
:::

Paid pricing scales with the events you send rather than the number of seats, so a small app pays little until it grows. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Amplitude provides SDKs for iOS in Swift, plus Android, React Native, Flutter, web, and a server-side API. On iOS you add the Swift SDK through Swift Package Manager, initialize it with an API key, and send named events with properties. Autocapture can record screen views and interactions, or you can instrument events by hand for tighter control.

The real work is not the SDK; it is deciding what to track. Plan an event and property naming scheme before you instrument, because renaming events after data accumulates is painful and past data does not backfill. Watch the monthly event volume too, since verbose autocapture can consume your allowance and push you toward a paid tier.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Amplitude free for a small app?
Yes, within limits. The Free plan allows up to 2 million events per month with unlimited seats and core product analytics (checked July 19, 2026). A small app can run on it indefinitely, and the Plus plan starts at $0 and scales only as your event volume grows.

### How is Amplitude different from Mixpanel?
Both are event-analytics tools covering funnels, retention, and cohorts. [Mixpanel](/tools/mixpanel) leans toward a lean, approachable reporting workflow priced by event volume, while Amplitude is a broader product platform that adds experiments, guides, and surveys alongside analytics, with a larger free event allowance and unlimited seats on every plan.

### Does Amplitude replace crash reporting?
No. Amplitude tells you what users did, not why the app broke. Pair it with a dedicated error monitor such as [Sentry](/tools/sentry) so you have both behavioral analytics and stack traces when something fails.

mixpanel.pricing_summary: Free for up to 1 million events per month; the Growth plan starts at $0 with 1 million events free and about $0.28 per 1,000 events after, and Enterprise is custom.
pros: The free plan covers up to 1 million events per month, which is enough for many small apps to run real analytics at no cost (checked July 19, 2026).
Usage-based Growth pricing at about $0.28 per 1,000 events after the free tier is easy to forecast, since cost rises in step with traffic rather than seat count.
Reports such as funnels, flows, and retention are approachable, so you can get answers without a dedicated analyst or a long onboarding.
An early-stage startup program can grant a free first year, which lowers the cost of adopting it before the app earns.
cons: Metering by event volume means chatty instrumentation directly raises your bill, so you have to be deliberate about what you send.
It reports on behavior only, so you still need separate crash and performance monitoring to know why something failed.
Session replay draws from its own monthly quota, which is easy to exhaust if you leave it enabled across the whole app.
Advanced governance, unlimited events, and compliance features sit behind quote-based Enterprise pricing rather than a published number.
mixpanel.body_markdown:
Mixpanel is event analytics for product teams: funnels, retention, flows, and cohorts built on the events your app sends. For a solo iOS developer, its appeal is approachability. You can answer where people drop off, or whether users who do one thing come back, without standing up your own data warehouse or learning a heavy platform first.

It fits well when product usage is a question you will keep returning to and you can keep a tidy event structure. If you only need install counts and a couple of headline numbers, it is more than the moment calls for, and a lighter tool such as [TelemetryDeck](/tools/telemetrydeck) covers that with less setup. Mixpanel is only as useful as the events you define, so sloppy instrumentation limits what you get out.

## Pricing
Mixpanel uses a freemium model metered by event volume, with a free tier and usage-based paid pricing.

:::comparison Mixpanel plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | $0, up to 1 million events per month | Up to 5 saved reports and 10,000 monthly session replays |
| Growth | Starts at $0; first 1 million events free, then about $0.28 per 1,000 events, with volume discounts | Unlimited reports, 20,000 monthly session replays, behavioral cohorts, custom events, and campaign reporting |
| Enterprise | Custom | Unlimited events, data governance and security, HIPAA tooling, and dedicated support |
:::

Eligible early-stage startups can also get the first year free through the startup program. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Mixpanel offers SDKs for iOS in Swift and Objective-C, plus Android, React Native, Flutter, web, and server-side libraries. On iOS you add the SDK through Swift Package Manager, initialize it with your project token, and call track for each event with properties. You can also route data through a customer-data platform if you already run one, which avoids wiring every event by hand.

Budget your time for taxonomy, not integration. Decide event and property names up front, because Mixpanel meters by event volume and inconsistent naming both wastes that allowance and fragments your reports. Session replay is available but counts against its own monthly quota, so enable it deliberately rather than leaving it on everywhere.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Mixpanel free for a small app?
Yes. The Free plan covers up to 1 million events per month with up to 5 saved reports and 10,000 monthly session replays (checked July 19, 2026). Beyond that, the Growth plan starts at $0 with the first 1 million events free and charges about $0.28 per 1,000 events after, so cost tracks usage.

### How is Mixpanel different from PostHog?
[PostHog](/tools/posthog) bundles analytics with session replay, feature flags, experiments, and surveys in one open-source platform, while Mixpanel stays focused on event analytics and reporting. If you want funnels and retention with a shallow learning curve, Mixpanel is simpler; if you want a wider toolkit from one account, PostHog consolidates more.

### Does the startup program change the price?
It can. Eligible early-stage startups can receive the first year free, which lowers the cost of adopting Mixpanel while you are pre-revenue (checked July 19, 2026). Confirm the current eligibility rules on the pricing page, since the thresholds change.

sentry.pricing_summary: Free Developer plan for 1 user; Team is $26 per month and Business is $80 per month (billed annually) with unlimited users, and Enterprise is custom.
pros: The free Developer plan covers a single user with 5,000 errors per month, which is enough to monitor one production app at no cost (checked July 19, 2026).
One workflow spans iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, web, and backend code, so errors that cross those boundaries land in the same place.
Issues connect to releases and, with debug symbols uploaded, to the offending line, which shortens the path from a crash report to a fix.
Paid plans include unlimited users at a flat monthly price, so adding collaborators does not raise the bill.
cons: Reports arrive unsymbolicated unless you upload dSYMs, so the setup is not truly finished until that step is wired into your build.
The breadth of errors, tracing, replay, logs, and monitors is more surface than a simple single-app crash reporter, and it takes time to configure well.
Event quotas span several dimensions at once, so a noisy release can consume errors, spans, or replays faster than you expect.
It monitors technical failures, not user behavior, so you still need a separate analytics tool to understand what people actually do.
sentry.body_markdown:
Sentry is error and performance monitoring that spans iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, web, and backend code. Beyond crashes it captures handled errors, release health, performance tracing, and session replay, and it ties each issue back to the release that introduced it. For a solo iOS developer, it turns vague one-star reviews into concrete, grouped, and actionable stack traces.

It fits especially well when errors cross boundaries, such as the app, a backend, and a marketing site, because one workflow then covers all of them. If the app already lives inside Firebase and you only want mobile crash reporting beside your other Firebase tools, [Firebase](/tools/firebase) Crashlytics is a lighter fit. Sentry is more than you need if app-store review monitoring is all you are after.

## Pricing
Sentry uses a freemium model with a free single-user tier and flat monthly paid plans that scale by event volume.

:::comparison Sentry plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Developer | $0 per month, limited to 1 user | 5,000 errors, 5 million spans, 50 session replays, and 1 cron monitor per month |
| Team | $26 per month billed annually, unlimited users | 50,000 errors, 5 million spans, 50 replays, third-party integrations, and optional Seer AI debugging |
| Business | $80 per month billed annually, unlimited users | Everything in Team plus unlimited metric monitors with anomaly detection, advanced quota management, and SAML with SCIM |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom allocations across all features, a technical account manager, dedicated support, and custom data retention |
:::

Paid plans include unlimited users and add capacity through event quotas across errors, spans, and replays. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Sentry provides an Apple SDK for iOS in Swift and Objective-C, plus SDKs for the other platforms it supports. On iOS you add the SDK through Swift Package Manager and initialize it early in app startup; the Sentry Wizard can automate much of that, including configuration and debug-symbol upload. Once running, it reports crashes, app hangs, and watchdog terminations automatically.

The step people skip is uploading debug symbols, the dSYM files. Without them, crash reports arrive unsymbolicated and hard to read, so wire dSYM upload into your build or fastlane pipeline early. Set release names during the build too, since release health and regression tracking depend on knowing which build an error came from.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Sentry free for a solo developer?
Yes, within limits. The Developer plan is free for a single user and includes 5,000 errors, 5 million spans, and 50 session replays per month (checked July 19, 2026). That is enough to monitor one app in production; you move to the paid Team plan when you need more volume or more than one user.

### How is Sentry different from Firebase Crashlytics?
Both catch crashes and make them fixable, but they differ in scope. [Firebase](/tools/firebase) Crashlytics is mobile-first and lives in the Firebase console next to Analytics and Remote Config, while Sentry is one error workflow that can also cover your backend, web, and API code with tracing and release health. Choose by how far beyond the mobile app your errors reach.

### What does Sentry cost as the app grows?
The Team plan is $26 per month and the Business plan is $80 per month, both billed annually with unlimited users, and each scales by event volume across errors, spans, and replays (checked July 19, 2026). Enterprise pricing is custom, so confirm current terms on the pricing page before you commit.

onesignal.pricing_summary: Free plan with unlimited mobile push; Growth starts at $19 per month plus per-user and per-send usage; Professional and Enterprise are custom.
pros: A genuinely free tier includes unlimited mobile push, so you can add push to a shipping iOS app and pay nothing until you need more email volume or journeys (checked July 19, 2026).
One SDK bundles push, in-app messages, email, and SMS, so you do not stitch together separate vendors as you add channels.
Segmentation and A/B testing are built in, so you target and test messages without writing your own delivery logic.
Native iOS support for rich notifications and Live Activities exists on higher plans, which matters when notifications are core to the app.
cons: The Growth plan layers per-active-user and per-send usage costs on top of the base fee, so a large free audience can turn into an unpredictable bill.
Professional and Enterprise pricing is quote-only, so budgeting past Growth means a sales conversation.
Adding the SDK pulls in a background service and a notification extension you maintain across iOS releases.
The free plan's journey and segment limits are tight, so lifecycle messaging of any depth pushes you onto a paid plan quickly.
onesignal.body_markdown:
OneSignal is messaging infrastructure for apps: push notifications, in-app messages, email, SMS, and journeys behind a single SDK and dashboard. For a solo iOS developer, the draw is that you can add reliable push to a shipping app in an afternoon and grow into lifecycle messaging later without swapping vendors. The free tier is generous enough that many small apps never pay for push at all.

It fits best when notifications or in-app messages are part of how the app works, not an afterthought. If you only need a single local reminder, Apple's own UserNotifications framework is enough and adding a third-party SDK is overkill. Teams that already run every channel through a broader engagement suite like [Braze](/tools/braze) will find the channels overlap.

## Pricing
:::comparison OneSignal plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | $0 per month | Unlimited mobile push, 10,000 monthly email sends, 1 journey, basic A/B testing, and 6 segments |
| Growth | From $19 per month plus usage | Adds per-active-user and per-send usage costs, more journeys, journey branching, and advanced in-app messaging |
| Professional | Custom, annual | Adds more journeys and steps, 24/7 support, CSV exports, and advanced personalization |
| Enterprise | Custom, annual | Adds frequency capping, full Live Activities access, an SLA, and a dedicated success manager |
:::

The Growth plan adds usage costs on top of the base fee, so cost scales with active users and message volume rather than staying flat. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
OneSignal provides SDKs for iOS in Swift, plus Android, React Native, Flutter, and web. On iOS you add the SDK through Swift Package Manager, enable the Push Notifications capability, and add a Notification Service Extension so rich media and delivery tracking work. You then upload your APNs key or certificate in the dashboard.

Expect the usual APNs friction rather than SDK friction. Provisioning profiles, the extension target, and testing on a real device are the slow parts, and the permission prompt must be requested at a sensible moment for opt-in rates. Live Activities and some advanced features sit on higher plans, so confirm your tier covers what the app needs.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is OneSignal free for push notifications?
Yes. The Free plan includes unlimited mobile push notifications at $0 per month, with limits on email sends, journeys, and segments (checked July 19, 2026). Many small apps run push entirely on the free tier.

### How is OneSignal different from Braze?
Both send cross-channel messages, but they target different buyers. [Braze](/tools/braze) is an enterprise engagement platform sold by quote and run by a marketing team, while OneSignal is self-serve, has a free tier, and suits a solo developer adding push without a sales process.

### Do I still need to configure APNs myself?
Yes. You create the APNs key in your Apple Developer account and upload it to OneSignal, and you request notification permission in the app. OneSignal handles delivery and targeting, but the Apple-side setup is yours.

braze.pricing_summary: Quote-only, sales-led pricing across 4 editions (Go, Select, Pro, and Enterprise) that scales by monthly active users; no public prices and no self-serve tier.
pros: Cross-channel orchestration across push, email, SMS, and in-app sits in one platform, so campaigns and journeys share the same user profiles and segments.
Predictive AI, experimentation, and personalization are built into the higher editions rather than bolted on afterward.
The platform is proven at large monthly-active-user counts, so it does not become the bottleneck as an app scales.
Deep data and warehouse integrations suit a team that already runs a wider marketing and analytics stack.
cons: Pricing is sales-led and scales by monthly active users, so there is no self-serve entry point and no public number to plan against (checked July 19, 2026).
The platform assumes a marketing or growth team to operate it, which a solo developer rarely has.
Onboarding and contracts are enterprise-weight, so time to first message is measured in weeks, not an afternoon.
The cost floor sits far above what a pre-revenue or early app can justify.
braze.body_markdown:
Braze is an enterprise customer-engagement platform for cross-channel campaigns across push, email, SMS, and in-app messaging. It centers on journeys, segmentation, and personalization driven by shared user profiles, and it is built to run at large monthly-active-user counts. It is the kind of platform a growth or lifecycle-marketing team operates, not a drop-in push SDK.

For a solo iOS developer, the honest answer is that Braze is off-audience. It is sales-led, priced for scale, and assumes a team to run campaigns and read the data. If you are adding push to an indie app, a self-serve tool like [OneSignal](/tools/onesignal) or Apple's own UserNotifications framework will fit your budget and your time far better. Braze becomes relevant only once an app has real scale, a marketing team, and cross-channel needs that justify the commitment.

## Pricing
Braze does not publish prices. It sells 4 editions, Braze Go, Braze Select, Braze Pro, and Braze Enterprise, with pricing that scales by monthly active users plus a credit system for messages and AI features. There are no public numbers and no self-serve tier, so any figure requires a sales conversation and a contract, and you should expect enterprise-level pricing well above what an early indie app can justify. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Braze offers a native iOS SDK in Swift, plus Android, web, and React Native, added through Swift Package Manager or CocoaPods. Integration involves wiring up user identification, event and attribute tracking, and the push and in-app messaging channels, then modeling your users so segments and journeys have data to work with.

The SDK is the small part. The larger effort is data planning: deciding which events and attributes to send, connecting Braze to your warehouse or customer data platform, and configuring channels like email and SMS with their own provider setup. This is a platform-level integration measured in weeks with a team, not an afternoon for one developer.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Braze suitable for a solo indie developer?
Usually not. Braze is enterprise software, priced by quote and scaled to large audiences, and it expects a marketing team to run it. A solo developer adding push is better served by a self-serve tool with a free tier.

### How is Braze different from OneSignal?
Both cover push, email, SMS, and in-app messaging. [OneSignal](/tools/onesignal) is self-serve with a free plan and suits individual developers, while Braze is a sales-led enterprise platform with deeper orchestration, predictive AI, and a price to match.

### Does Braze publish its pricing?
No. Pricing is quote-only and scales by monthly active users across 4 editions, so there is no public price list and no way to sign up without contacting sales (checked July 19, 2026).

appfigures.pricing_summary: Paid plans from $9.99 per month (Connect) through Monitor, Optimize, Boost, and Amplify, each covering 5 apps with more keywords and intelligence per tier; a free trial is available.
pros: The entry Connect plan starts at $9.99 per month, so download, revenue, and review tracking is affordable for a single app from launch (checked July 19, 2026).
One dashboard combines store analytics, keyword ranks, and review replies, so you do not run separate tools for revenue and ASO.
Every plan includes your apps and adds keyword allowances as you climb tiers, so the price maps to how much ASO work you actually do.
Apple Ads intelligence and deeper market data are available on higher tiers if paid acquisition becomes part of the plan.
cons: Keyword allowances are modest on the lower plans, so serious keyword research pushes you toward the $149.99 Optimize tier or higher.
Competitor and full app-intelligence data unlock only on the expensive tiers, which are priced for agencies rather than solo developers.
The breadth of the dashboard is more than an app that only needs basic download numbers requires.
Plans cover 5 apps with additional apps charged separately, so a larger portfolio adds up.
appfigures.body_markdown:
Appfigures is app store intelligence for indie developers and small teams: download and revenue analytics, keyword and rank tracking, review monitoring, and Apple Ads data in one dashboard. It connects to your App Store Connect account for your own numbers and estimates data for apps you do not own. For a solo iOS developer, the appeal is a single affordable place to watch both revenue and ASO instead of juggling separate tools.

It fits from launch because the entry plan is cheap and every tier includes your apps. If you only need basic download counts, App Store Connect already provides those for free, and the deeper keyword and competitor features are more than a first release needs. The heavier tiers are priced for agencies rather than individuals.

## Pricing
:::comparison Appfigures plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Connect | $9.99 per month ($7.99 annual) | 5 apps, 25 keywords, download and revenue tracking, subscription analytics, ranks, and unlimited review replies |
| Monitor | $44.99 per month ($35.99 annual) | Adds 100 keywords, daily rank updates, popularity scores, and an ASO snapshot |
| Optimize | $149.99 per month ($119.99 annual) | Adds 500 keywords, 1 premium competitor, competitor keyword tracking, and hourly rank updates |
| Boost and Amplify | From $599.99 per month | Higher keyword and competitor limits plus broad app-intelligence and market data for agencies |
:::

Every plan covers 5 apps with additional apps at $1.99 each, and a free trial is available. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Appfigures is a web tool, so there is no SDK to add to your app. You connect your App Store Connect and Google Play accounts through their APIs to pull your own downloads, revenue, and reviews, then add the keywords and competitors you want to track. Setup is account linking and configuration rather than code.

Two things to expect. Store data can lag by a day because it depends on Apple's and Google's reporting, and keyword and competitor estimates are modeled figures, useful for direction rather than exact truth. Budget your keyword list to your plan, since allowances are the main limit on the cheaper tiers.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is Appfigures different from AppTweak and AppFollow?
All three cover ASO, but with different centers of gravity. Appfigures pairs store analytics with ASO, [AppTweak](/tools/apptweak) is a deeper keyword-and-competitor research tool, and [AppFollow](/tools/appfollow) focuses on review management and reputation. Appfigures is a natural single dashboard for a solo developer who wants revenue and ASO together.

### Is Appfigures affordable for one app?
Yes. The Connect plan starts at $9.99 per month and includes 5 apps, so a single-app developer pays little for download, revenue, and review tracking (checked July 19, 2026). Serious keyword research pushes you to higher tiers.

### Do I need to add an SDK?
No. Appfigures reads data through the App Store Connect and Google Play APIs, so you connect accounts rather than ship code. That also means it cannot see in-app behavior the stores do not report.

apptweak.pricing_summary: Published ASO plans in euros from 79 euros per month (Essential) through Grow and Grow Plus, each with 1 seat; Enterprise is custom, and a 7-day free trial is available.
pros: Pricing is published in tiers from 79 euros per month, so you can evaluate the cost before talking to sales (checked July 19, 2026).
Keyword research, competitor analysis, and rank tracking sit in one ASO-focused workspace rather than being scattered across tools.
Historical data windows widen with each tier, which helps when you need to see how ranks moved across a season.
A 7-day free trial lets you test the keyword tools against your own app before committing.
cons: Plans are priced in euros and start higher than entry analytics tools, so it is a real monthly commitment for a single indie app.
Each published plan includes only 1 seat, so collaborating means the Enterprise tier or added cost.
AI visibility, the ASO agent, and creative tools are gated to the quote-based Enterprise tier.
It is ASO intelligence only, so you still need separate tools for revenue and crash reporting.
apptweak.body_markdown:
AppTweak is app store optimization intelligence: keyword research, rank tracking, competitor analysis, and store audits in one workspace. It leans academic about ASO, with organic-install estimates per keyword, keyword-impact scoring, and long historical windows. For a solo iOS developer who takes keywords seriously, it is a focused research tool rather than a general analytics dashboard.

It fits when ASO is a deliberate, ongoing effort and you want data to back keyword decisions. If you only need to check ranks occasionally or want revenue analytics too, a broader tool like [Appfigures](/tools/appfigures) covers more ground for less. AppTweak is ASO intelligence only, so it sits alongside, not instead of, your analytics and crash tooling.

## Pricing
:::comparison AppTweak plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Essential | 79 euros per month (949 euros annual) | 500 keywords, 1 seat, 6 months of history, keyword research, live searches, and competitor analysis |
| Grow | 249 euros per month (2,990 euros annual) | 1,500 keywords, 12 months of history, organic installs per keyword, impact analysis, and Slack alerts |
| Grow Plus | 499 euros per month (5,990 euros annual) | 3,000 keywords, 24 months of history, and higher platform limits |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom keywords and seats, all-time history, AI visibility, the ASO agent, and creative tools |
:::

Prices are published in euros, every named plan includes 1 seat, and a 7-day free trial is available. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
AppTweak is a web platform, so there is nothing to install in your app. You add your apps, choose the countries and stores to track, and build the keyword lists and competitor sets you want to monitor. The work is configuration and ongoing research rather than a code integration.

Expect a learning curve. The value is in the depth of the metrics, so it takes time to learn what keyword-impact scores and organic-install estimates mean for your decisions. Those figures are modeled from public signals, so treat them as strong direction rather than exact counts, and mind that the single seat on published plans limits collaboration.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is AppTweak different from Appfigures and AppFollow?
All three touch ASO. AppTweak is a deep keyword-and-competitor research tool, [Appfigures](/tools/appfigures) combines store analytics with lighter ASO, and [AppFollow](/tools/appfollow) centers on review management. If keyword research is the priority, AppTweak goes deepest; if you want revenue and ASO together, Appfigures is the broader single tool.

### Does AppTweak publish its prices?
Yes. Essential, Grow, and Grow Plus are listed in euros starting at 79 euros per month, with only Enterprise quoted by sales (checked July 19, 2026). A 7-day free trial lets you test first.

### Is AppTweak worth it for a single indie app?
It can be, if ASO is a real focus. The entry plan is a meaningful monthly commitment, so it pays off when you actively research and iterate on keywords rather than checking ranks now and then.

appfollow.pricing_summary: A free plan plus paid tiers that scale by apps, review replies, keywords, seats, and integrations, topped by a custom Enterprise tier; concrete paid prices were not published on the page at checking time.
pros: Reviews from the App Store, Google Play, and other stores land in one inbox with sentiment analysis, so you reply and track feedback in a single place.
A free plan exists for a small app, covering a couple of apps with a limited number of review replies and keywords per month (checked July 19, 2026).
Automation and AI-assisted replies handle repetitive review responses in multiple languages.
Integrations push reviews and alerts into Slack, Zendesk, and other tools your support flow already uses.
cons: Concrete paid-tier prices were not shown on the pricing page without contact, so budgeting past the free plan is hard to do up front (checked July 19, 2026).
The platform leans toward review and reputation management, so its ASO depth is lighter than a dedicated keyword tool.
Review-reply and keyword allowances on the free plan are low, so an active app outgrows it quickly.
Its value depends on having enough review volume to manage, which a brand-new app does not yet have.
appfollow.body_markdown:
AppFollow is app reputation management: it pulls reviews and ratings from the App Store, Google Play, and other stores into one inbox, adds sentiment analysis and AI-assisted replies, and layers on ASO monitoring and reporting. For a solo iOS developer, the value is a single place to read, answer, and track user feedback across stores instead of checking each console by hand.

It fits once an app has enough review volume that responding and spotting trends becomes real work. A brand-new app with a handful of reviews does not need it yet. Its ASO features are lighter than a dedicated research tool, so if keywords are your main focus, [AppTweak](/tools/apptweak) or [Appfigures](/tools/appfigures) will go deeper.

## Pricing
AppFollow offers a free plan plus paid tiers that scale by the number of apps, review replies, keywords tracked, seats, and integrations, with a custom Enterprise tier at the top. The pricing page did not expose concrete tier prices at the time of checking, and third-party listings disagree on the numbers, so this guide does not quote figures that cannot be confirmed. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
AppFollow is a web platform with no SDK to add to your app. You connect your App Store Connect and Google Play accounts so it can pull reviews, ratings, and store data, then set up the integrations, such as Slack, Zendesk, or Helpshift, that route reviews into your support flow. Setup is account linking and workflow configuration rather than code.

Expect the value to depend on your review volume and on how you wire the integrations. Automated and AI-assisted replies save time but need review and tone-setting so responses do not read as generic, and store data can lag by a day because it relies on Apple's and Google's reporting.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is AppFollow different from Appfigures and AppTweak?
All three overlap on ASO, but AppFollow's center is review and reputation management with a shared reply inbox. [Appfigures](/tools/appfigures) pairs store analytics with ASO, and [AppTweak](/tools/apptweak) is a deeper keyword-research tool. Choose AppFollow when responding to and analyzing reviews across stores is the main job.

### Does AppFollow have a free plan?
Yes. There is a free tier covering a small number of apps with limited review replies and keywords per month, enough to try the inbox before paying (checked July 19, 2026). Concrete paid-tier prices were not published on the page at checking time.

### Can AppFollow replace a dedicated ASO tool?
For light ASO monitoring and reporting, it can. For deep keyword research, organic-install estimates, and competitor analysis, a focused tool like [AppTweak](/tools/apptweak) is stronger. Many teams use AppFollow for reviews and add a research tool when ASO becomes a priority.

framer.pricing_summary: Free on a Framer subdomain, Basic at $10 per month billed yearly for a custom domain, Pro at $30 per month billed yearly, and custom Enterprise.
pros: Publishing to Framer's own hosting means you do not set up a server, a CDN, or SSL certificates yourself.
The Basic plan connects a custom domain for $10 per month billed yearly, which is enough for a single app landing page (checked July 19, 2026).
Built-in animation and interaction tools let a solo developer produce a polished page without writing front-end code.
Form elements can feed an email tool, so a waitlist page needs no separate form service.
cons: The freeform canvas is designed around visual editing, so a developer who prefers writing layout in code may find it slower than expected.
The CMS is lighter than a dedicated content platform, which shows if you plan a large, structured blog.
The advertised prices are annual-billing rates, so paying monthly costs more than the headline figure.
Each additional editor is a paid seat, which adds up if more than one person edits the site.
framer.body_markdown:
Framer is a visual website builder for marketing sites and landing pages that publishes to its own hosting. For a solo iOS developer, it is a way to get an app landing page or a waitlist live in an afternoon without touching HTML or CSS, using a canvas editor, templates, and built-in animation. It fits the moment you need a public URL for your app, whether that is a pre-launch waitlist, a download page, or a small marketing site.

It fits less well when the site is content-heavy. Framer's CMS is lighter than a dedicated content platform, and [Webflow](/tools/webflow) gives you more control once a large, structured blog is involved. Framer also does not build native mobile UI; it is for the web page around your app, not the app itself.

## Pricing
Framer has a free tier plus paid site plans, with the headline prices billed yearly.

:::comparison Framer plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | $0 | A Framer subdomain, 1 GB bandwidth, unlimited design pages, and up to 3 editors, without a custom domain |
| Basic | $10 per month billed yearly | A free custom domain, built-in SEO, 2 CMS collections, 50 GB bandwidth, and 30 pages |
| Pro | $30 per month billed yearly | A custom domain, 10 CMS collections, 100 GB bandwidth, a staging environment, and branching with previews |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom credits, unlimited editors, SSO, SCIM, and an uptime guarantee |
:::

For a single app landing page, the Basic plan covers a custom domain and enough pages, while the Free plan only publishes on a Framer subdomain. Monthly billing costs more than the annual rates shown. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Framer is web-only, so setup means building and publishing a page, not adding an SDK to your app. You start from a template or a blank canvas, edit visually, and publish to Framer hosting. Connecting a custom domain is a matter of pointing DNS records, which Framer lists for you. A waitlist form can be built with Framer's form elements and routed to an email tool such as [Kit](/tools/kit) or [beehiiv](/tools/beehiiv).

Two gotchas: the Free plan does not allow a custom domain, so a real launch needs at least Basic; and the freeform canvas rewards designers, so a developer who wants a coded layout may need time to adjust. Expect an afternoon to get a clean one-page site live.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Framer or Webflow better for an app landing page?
Both publish fast, hosted marketing sites without code. [Webflow](/tools/webflow) gives finer control over layout and a stronger CMS for content-heavy sites, while Framer leans toward quick visual design and animation. For a single landing page or waitlist, Framer is usually faster to ship.

### Can I collect waitlist emails with Framer?
Yes. Framer's form elements capture email addresses and route them to an email platform such as [beehiiv](/tools/beehiiv) or [Kit](/tools/kit), so the signup stays on the page while the list lives in a tool built for sending.

### Do I need a paid plan to use a custom domain?
Yes. The Free plan publishes only on a Framer subdomain. To connect your own domain you need the Basic plan or higher (checked July 19, 2026).

webflow.pricing_summary: Free previews on a webflow.io subdomain, Basic at $15 per month billed yearly for a static site, Premium at $25 per month billed yearly with a CMS, and custom Enterprise.
pros: The visual designer exposes real CSS layout controls, so a developer gets precise responsive control without hand-writing markup.
The included CMS on the Premium plan supports a blog, a changelog, and dynamic pages without a separate content service.
Hosting, SSL, and a global CDN are handled by Webflow, so there is no server to maintain.
Native forms capture submissions and can forward them to an email platform through webhooks.
cons: The split between Workspace plans and Site plans is confusing at first and means you reason about two subscriptions.
The designer has a steeper learning curve than template-first builders, so the first site takes longer to complete.
Hosting a live site with a custom domain requires a paid Site plan, so the free tier only serves previews.
The May 2026 plan changes merged the old CMS and Business tiers into one Premium plan, so older pricing guides may be out of date.
webflow.body_markdown:
Webflow is a visual website builder with a real CMS, hosting, and forms, aimed at marketing sites that need structure. For a solo iOS developer, it is a way to build an app landing page, a blog or changelog, and a waitlist page that you control down to the layout, then host it on Webflow's infrastructure.

It fits less well when you only need one static page live quickly. Webflow has a steeper learning curve than a template-first builder, and it separates the Workspace plan you design in from the Site plan you publish. If speed on a single page is the goal, [Framer](/tools/framer) is usually faster; Webflow earns its keep once you have content that grows.

## Pricing
Webflow splits Site plans, which host a published site, from Workspace plans, which cover designing and collaboration; the May 2026 update simplified Site plans into Basic and Premium.

:::comparison Webflow Site plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Starter | $0 | Build and publish on a webflow.io subdomain, without a custom domain, for previews and testing |
| Basic | $15 per month billed yearly | A custom domain and hosting for a static site, without CMS features |
| Premium | $25 per month billed yearly | Everything in Basic plus 20,000 CMS items and 40 CMS collections for dynamic content |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom limits, advanced security, and dedicated support |
:::

A free Starter Workspace plus one paid Site plan is the common setup for a solo builder. Monthly billing costs more than the annual rates shown, at $25 per month for Basic and $39 per month for Premium. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Webflow is web-only, so integration means designing and publishing a site rather than adding a mobile SDK. You build in the visual designer, connect a custom domain by updating DNS records, and publish to Webflow hosting. A waitlist form uses Webflow's native form elements, which store submissions and can forward them to an email tool such as [Kit](/tools/kit) through a webhook.

The main gotchas are the two-plan model and the learning curve: hosting a live site requires a paid Site plan, so the free tier is only for previews, and the designer exposes the box model, flexbox, and grid directly. Expect a longer first build than a template tool, and faster edits afterward.

## Frequently asked questions
### Should I choose Webflow or Framer for an app site?
Both are hosted, no-server website builders. [Framer](/tools/framer) is faster for a single visually driven landing page, while Webflow gives more layout control and a stronger CMS, which matters once you add a blog or changelog.

### Why does Webflow charge twice?
Webflow separates the Workspace plan, which covers designing and collaboration, from the Site plan, which hosts a published site. A solo developer can stay on the free Workspace and pay only for one Site plan per live site.

### Can Webflow handle my app's blog and waitlist together?
Yes. The Premium Site plan includes a CMS with 20,000 items and 40 collections, which is enough for a blog, a changelog, and dynamic pages, while native forms capture waitlist signups (checked July 19, 2026).

beehiiv.pricing_summary: Free up to 2,500 subscribers, Scale from $43 per month and Max from $96 per month (each up to 100,000 subscribers), plus custom Enterprise.
pros: The free Launch plan carries up to 2,500 subscribers with unlimited sends, so an early waitlist costs nothing (checked July 19, 2026).
A built-in signup form and hosted website mean a waitlist can launch without a separate landing page.
Paid subscriptions and an ad network are built in if you later want the newsletter to earn revenue.
Domain authentication is guided, which helps a first-time sender reach the inbox.
cons: Paid plan prices rise with subscriber count, so costs grow as the list grows.
It does not send transactional app email, so you still need a separate service for receipts and resets.
Monetization features such as the ad network add surface area you do not need if you only want a waitlist.
Removing beehiiv branding requires the Max plan, which is a large step up from the free tier.
beehiiv.body_markdown:
beehiiv is a newsletter platform: it captures subscribers, sends email, and hosts a simple companion website for your posts. For a solo iOS developer, the value is a place to grow a waitlist or a launch audience and keep them warm with updates before and after release, without stitching together a form, a list, and a sender. Signup forms and a landing page are built in, so a waitlist can go live without a separate site.

It fits when the point is an audience you email regularly. If you only need transactional messages from your app, such as receipts or password resets, beehiiv is the wrong tool; that is a job for an email API. For pure list building and creator-style newsletters, [Kit](/tools/kit) is the closest alternative.

## Pricing
beehiiv has a free tier plus paid plans whose prices scale with your subscriber count.

:::comparison beehiiv plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Launch | $0 | Up to 2,500 subscribers, unlimited sends, a custom domain, a website, and basic analytics |
| Scale | From $43 per month, or $517 per year | Up to 100,000 subscribers, 3 publications, the ad network, paid subscriptions, automations, and 3 team seats |
| Max | From $96 per month, or $1,151 per year | Up to 100,000 subscribers, 10 publications, branding removal, unlimited seats, and priority support |
| Enterprise | Custom | Higher subscriber limits, the Send API, dedicated IP addresses, and SSO |
:::

The free Launch plan covers up to 2,500 subscribers with unlimited sends, which is enough for many app waitlists before launch. Paid plan prices scale with your subscriber count, so the figures above are starting points. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
beehiiv is a web platform, so setup is creating a publication, not adding an SDK. You set a sending name and a reply address, then either use a beehiiv subdomain or connect your own domain by adding the DNS records beehiiv provides, including the records that authenticate your sending. A signup form can live on beehiiv's own landing page or be embedded on a site you built in [Framer](/tools/framer) or [Webflow](/tools/webflow).

Two things to plan for: deliverability depends on completing domain authentication, so do not skip the DNS step; and beehiiv is built around the newsletter, so it does not send transactional app email. Expect an hour or two to configure a publication, a domain, and a first signup form.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is beehiiv or Kit better for an app waitlist?
Both capture subscribers and send newsletters. [Kit](/tools/kit) leans toward creator automations and selling digital products, while beehiiv emphasizes audience growth, a built-in website, and ad or subscription monetization. For a simple pre-launch waitlist either works; the choice comes down to whether you want beehiiv's growth tools or Kit's automation depth.

### How many subscribers can I have for free?
The Launch plan is free up to 2,500 subscribers with unlimited email sends (checked July 19, 2026), which covers many waitlists before an app ships.

### Can beehiiv send my app's transactional email?
No. beehiiv is a newsletter and audience tool, not a transactional email service. Receipts, password resets, and similar app messages need a transactional email API instead.

kit.pricing_summary: Free up to 10,000 subscribers with 1 automation; Creator from $33 per month and Creator Pro from $66 per month, both scaling with subscriber count.
pros: The free Newsletter plan allows up to 10,000 subscribers with unlimited forms and broadcasts, so a growing waitlist can cost nothing (checked July 19, 2026).
Visual automations let a welcome or launch sequence run without manual sending once it is set up.
Hosted forms and landing pages mean a waitlist can go live without building a separate site.
Selling digital products is built in, which is useful if you later offer a paid download or guide.
cons: The free plan allows only 1 automation, so any real welcome sequence pushes you to a paid plan.
Paid plan prices rise with subscriber count, so a large list becomes expensive once you need paid features.
It does not handle transactional app email, so receipts and resets need a separate service.
The creator and product-selling focus is more than a developer who only wants a plain waitlist requires.
kit.body_markdown:
Kit, formerly ConvertKit, is email marketing built for creators: email capture forms, landing pages, broadcasts, and visual automations, plus tools to sell digital products. For a solo iOS developer, it is a straightforward way to build a waitlist or a launch list and then automate a welcome sequence, so new signups hear from you without manual sending. Forms and landing pages are included, so a waitlist can run on Kit alone.
