It fits list building and nurture sequences rather than app system email. If you need transactional messages from your app, Kit is not the tool. For a close comparison, [beehiiv](/tools/beehiiv) covers similar ground with more emphasis on a public website and audience growth.

## Pricing
Kit has a free tier plus paid plans whose prices scale with your subscriber count.

:::comparison Kit plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Newsletter | $0 | Up to 10,000 subscribers, unlimited forms and landing pages, unlimited broadcasts, and 1 automation |
| Creator | From $33 per month at 1,000 subscribers | Unlimited automations and sequences, A/B tested subject lines, and branding removal |
| Creator Pro | From $66 per month at 1,000 subscribers | Subscriber scoring and signals, engagement analytics, a referral system, and unlimited team users |
:::

The free Newsletter plan allows up to 10,000 subscribers, though it limits you to a single automation, so a plain waitlist can run at no cost for a long time. Paid plan prices scale with subscriber count, and annual billing is discounted. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Kit is a web tool, so setup means creating forms and lists, not adding an SDK. You build a form or landing page, then either embed the form on your own site or use Kit's hosted landing page. Connecting a custom sending domain involves adding DNS records that authenticate your email, which improves deliverability. Signups can flow in from a page you built in [Framer](/tools/framer) or [Webflow](/tools/webflow), or straight from a Kit landing page.

Plan for two things: the free plan's single-automation limit means a multi-step welcome sequence needs the Creator plan; and, as with any sender, completing domain authentication is what keeps your email out of spam folders. Expect under an hour to publish a form and a first landing page.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Kit or beehiiv better for a launch list?
Both handle signups, broadcasts, and automations. [beehiiv](/tools/beehiiv) puts more weight on a public website, audience growth, and monetization, while Kit focuses on automation, sequences, and selling digital products. For a waitlist that becomes a nurture sequence, Kit's automations are the draw; for a public newsletter with a site, beehiiv fits well.

### Is Kit free for a small waitlist?
Yes. The Newsletter plan is free up to 10,000 subscribers, with unlimited forms, landing pages, and broadcasts, though only 1 automation (checked July 19, 2026). That covers many app waitlists before launch.

### Is Kit the same as ConvertKit?
Yes. Kit is the renamed ConvertKit; the product and the account are the same, and only the name changed.

appscreens.pricing_summary: Free plan for the basics, then paid Pro and Scale plans (billed monthly or annually) that add projects, localizations, and a client-work license; exact figures could not be confirmed from the official page on July 19, 2026.
pros: It runs entirely in the browser with no design tool required, so you can assemble framed, captioned store screenshots without opening Figma or Sketch.
It organizes work into reusable projects, which makes refreshing a listing for a new release faster than rebuilding every frame by hand.
The localization workflow keeps one layout across many languages, so translated captions drop into the same frames instead of being rebuilt per locale.
It exports store-ready sizes for both App Store Connect and Google Play, which removes the manual resizing step before upload.
cons: It does not capture the raw screenshots for you, so you still produce the underlying app captures yourself.
Localization arranges the layout but does not translate your caption copy, so you supply the translated strings.
Public pricing figures were not readable from the official page on the day checked, so confirm current plan costs before you commit.
As a creative-only tool it does no keyword research, so the words that drive App Store search still need a separate process.
appscreens.body_markdown:
AppScreens is a browser-based screenshot builder for the App Store and Google Play. It gives you device frames, captions, templates, and localized layouts, then exports store-ready sizes so you can prepare a listing without a design tool. For a solo iOS developer, the appeal is turning a set of raw captures into a polished, framed listing in an afternoon, and keeping many-language variants in one place.

It fits when you are preparing or refreshing a store listing and want localized frames quickly. It does not fit if you need App Store keyword research, analytics, or any backend work, because it is a creative tool only. If you already have a screenshot workflow you like in a design tool, the payoff is smaller. [Screenshots Pro](/tools/screenshots-pro) is a close alternative worth comparing.

## Pricing
AppScreens uses a freemium model (checked July 19, 2026). There is a free plan that covers basic layouts and export, then paid Pro and Scale plans, billed monthly or annually, that unlock more projects, localization, custom fonts, multi-layer editing, and automatic App Store uploads. The higher Scale tier adds unlimited projects and an extended license for client work. The official pricing page is a JavaScript application whose exact figures could not be read on the day checked, so the specific plan costs are not stated here. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
AppScreens is web-based, so there is no SDK and nothing to add to your app. You import your own screenshots or start from a device frame, add captions, choose a device, and export every required App Store and Google Play size at once. Effort is low, and a first listing is the work of an afternoon.

Plan for the parts it does not do. It arranges and styles screenshots you already have, so you still capture the raw screens from the simulator, a device, or a tool such as [fastlane](/tools/fastlane). Localization lays out translated captions but does not translate them, so you provide the translated copy. Keep your frame sizes matched to the current App Store Connect requirements before you export.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is AppScreens different from Screenshots Pro?
Both are browser-based store screenshot builders with templates, device frames, captions, and localization. [Screenshots Pro](/tools/screenshots-pro) publishes a clear tiered price with a permanently free plan, while AppScreens organizes work into projects and leans on localization workflows for many-language listings. The choice usually comes down to which editor and template set you prefer.

### Does AppScreens capture screenshots from my app?
No. It arranges and styles screenshots you already have. You take the raw captures from the simulator or a device, or generate them with [fastlane](/tools/fastlane), then bring them in to add frames, captions, and localized layouts.

### Is there a free plan?
Yes. AppScreens offers a free plan that covers basic layouts and export, so you can prepare a simple listing at no cost, with paid plans adding more projects, localizations, and features (checked July 19, 2026). Confirm the current limits before you rely on them.

screenshots-pro.pricing_summary: Free Basic plan with the full editor and every device size, then Standard at $19 per month and Extended at $49 per month, with annual billing about 35 percent cheaper.
pros: The free Basic plan includes every device size, all templates, and the full editor, so you can produce a complete first listing without paying.
Smart export renders every required App Store and Google Play size in one pass, which removes manual resizing.
The Extended plan grants an extended license and the right to bill clients, which makes it viable for contract or agency work.
The paid features are the ones you tend to need late, such as localization and custom fonts, so cost lines up with launch rather than day one.
cons: Localization, custom fonts, and 3D angles sit behind paid tiers, so a polished multi-language listing is not free.
It frames and styles images but does not capture them, so producing the raw screenshots remains your job.
API access is available only on the higher-priced Extended plan, which is a jump if you want to automate exports.
Exact annual prices are shown only as a percentage discount, so the yearly total needs a manual check before you buy.
screenshots-pro.body_markdown:
Screenshots Pro is a browser-based screenshot builder for the App Store and Google Play. It offers templates, device frames, captions, 3D angles, and a smart export that produces every required size at once. For a solo iOS developer, the draw is that the full editor and every device size are usable on the free plan, so you can build a first listing without paying anything.

It fits when you want polished, framed screenshots quickly and may only need paid features closer to launch. It does not fit if you need App Store keyword tracking or analytics, because it is a creative tool only. If you ship in one language and one visual style, the free plan may be all you ever need. [AppScreens](/tools/appscreens) is a close alternative built around projects and localization.

## Pricing
Screenshots Pro uses a freemium model with two paid tiers.

:::comparison Screenshots Pro plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Basic | $0 | All devices, all templates, the full editor, auto-save, and smart export to every store size |
| Standard | $19 per month, with roughly 35 percent off on annual billing | Everything in Basic plus 3D angles, template storage, custom fonts, localization, and a standard license |
| Extended | $49 per month | Everything in Standard plus API access, an extended license, and the right to charge end clients |
:::

The free Basic plan is unusually complete for a screenshot tool, which makes Standard mainly about localization and custom typography. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Screenshots Pro is web-based, so there is no SDK. You import your own captures, arrange them on templates, adjust device frames and captions, then use smart export to generate every App Store and Google Play size in a single pass. Effort is low, and a first listing takes an afternoon at most.

Plan for 2 things. Like any builder, it does not take the raw screenshots, so you capture those from the simulator, a device, or [fastlane](/tools/fastlane) first. API access and the right to bill clients live only on the Extended plan, so contract or agency work needs the Extended plan.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is Screenshots Pro different from AppScreens?
Both are browser-based store screenshot builders. [AppScreens](/tools/appscreens) organizes work into projects with a localization-first workflow, while Screenshots Pro gives the full editor and every device size on a permanently free plan and puts localization, custom fonts, and 3D angles on the paid tiers. Choose on editor feel and how many languages you ship.

### Is the free plan good enough to launch?
Often, yes. The Basic plan includes every device, all templates, the full editor, and smart export at $0 (checked July 19, 2026), so a single-language listing can ship without paying. You move up mainly for localization, custom fonts, or API access.

### Do I still need to capture my own screenshots?
Yes. Screenshots Pro styles and frames images you supply. You capture the raw screens from the simulator or a device, or automate them with [fastlane](/tools/fastlane), then bring them in for framing, captions, and export.

codemagic.pricing_summary: Free tier with 500 macOS build minutes per month, then pay-as-you-go from $0.045 per minute on Linux or $0.095 per minute on the M2 Mac, plus fixed annual plans from $3,990 and enterprise from $12,000.
pros: The free 500 macOS minutes per month let a solo developer run real iOS builds and TestFlight uploads at no cost.
Pay-as-you-go billing ties spend to how often you build, so an app with a slow release cadence does not pay for idle capacity.
Configuration lives in a codemagic.yaml file in your repository, so the pipeline is version-controlled alongside the app.
It can call fastlane lanes, so existing local automation carries into CI without a rewrite.
cons: macOS build minutes are billed at a real per-minute rate, so slow or frequent builds add up quickly.
Each extra concurrent build costs $49, so running pipelines in parallel raises the price meaningfully.
Fixed-price and enterprise plans start in the thousands per year, which only makes sense at a heavy build volume.
iOS code signing on hosted machines takes deliberate setup before the first green build.
codemagic.body_markdown:
Codemagic is hosted mobile CI/CD. It runs your builds, tests, code signing, and store uploads on managed macOS and Linux machines, so you do not maintain a build server or keep a Mac awake for releases. For a solo iOS developer, the appeal is renting a Mac in the cloud for builds and TestFlight uploads, configured in YAML or a workflow editor, with a free monthly allowance to start.

It fits when local builds and manual uploads start eating time, or when you want a build on every push without babysitting a machine. It does not fit if you release rarely and a local [fastlane](/tools/fastlane) run is already enough, because hosted CI is then overhead you do not need. [Bitrise](/tools/bitrise) is the closest alternative to weigh against it.

## Pricing
Codemagic combines a free tier with usage-based and fixed plans.

:::comparison Codemagic plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free | $0 | 500 macOS M2 build minutes per month, 1 concurrency, and 1 team member |
| Pay as you go | Usage-based, from $0.045 per minute on Linux and $0.095 per minute on the M2 Mac | Unlimited minutes billed per minute, unlimited team members, and extra concurrency at $49 each |
| Fixed price | From $3,990 per year for the M2 machine | Unlimited minutes, 3 concurrent builds, and unlimited team members for a set annual fee |
| Enterprise | From $12,000 per year | Custom machines, support, and configuration for high-volume teams |
:::

For a solo developer the free 500 minutes cover a light release cadence, and pay as you go keeps cost tied to how often you build. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Codemagic supports native iOS and Android, Flutter, React Native, and Unity. You connect a Git repository and define builds in a codemagic.yaml file or the workflow editor; the iOS path covers signing with an App Store Connect API key, building the archive, and uploading to TestFlight or the App Store. Expect a moderate first setup, roughly an afternoon for a working iOS pipeline.

Two gotchas are worth planning for. iOS code signing is the usual stumbling block, so set up the App Store Connect API key and certificates early. Build minutes on macOS machines are real money, so cache dependencies and avoid rebuilding on every trivial commit.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is Codemagic different from Bitrise?
Both are hosted mobile CI/CD with macOS machines and store deployment. [Bitrise](/tools/bitrise) uses a step-based visual workflow and a credit system on fixed monthly plans, while Codemagic centers on a YAML config with a free monthly minute allowance and pay-as-you-go billing. Codemagic is often the lighter starting point for a solo developer, while Bitrise leans toward a large step library.

### Do I still need fastlane with Codemagic?
Not necessarily, but they pair well. Codemagic can sign, build, and upload on its own, yet many pipelines call [fastlane](/tools/fastlane) lanes for finer control over metadata, screenshots, or signing. Running the same lanes locally and in CI keeps behavior consistent.

### Is the free tier enough for one app?
For a light cadence, yes. The free plan includes 500 macOS build minutes per month (checked July 19, 2026), which covers occasional releases and TestFlight builds. Frequent builds, or a need for parallel runs, is where pay as you go or a fixed plan starts to matter.

bitrise.pricing_summary: Free Hobby plan with 300 build credits per month for 1 app, then Starter at $89 per month billed annually and Pro at $200 per month billed annually, with custom Enterprise pricing.
pros: The visual step library lets you assemble a build, test, and release pipeline without writing most of the scripting yourself.
Bitrise-managed code signing can handle certificates and profiles, which removes one of the more painful parts of iOS CI.
The free Hobby plan runs real iOS builds, so you can prove out a pipeline before paying.
Steps can run fastlane lanes, so existing automation moves into CI without being rewritten.
cons: Paid plans start at $89 per month, which is a higher floor than pay-as-you-go rivals for a low build volume.
Builds are metered in credits, so estimating a monthly cost takes attention to machine size and build length.
The free Hobby plan is limited to 1 app and 1 member, so a second project needs a paid plan.
The large step catalog adds surface area to learn compared with a plain YAML pipeline.
bitrise.body_markdown:
Bitrise is a mobile-focused CI/CD platform built around a library of prebuilt steps for building, testing, signing, and distributing iOS and Android apps. For a solo iOS developer, the draw is assembling a pipeline from visual steps rather than writing everything by hand, on hosted macOS machines, with a free Hobby plan to start.

It fits when you want a guided, step-based workflow and expect to grow into a team. It does not fit if your release is simple enough for a local [fastlane](/tools/fastlane) script, because hosted CI is then added cost and setup. Its paid plans also start higher than pay-as-you-go rivals, so a very light build cadence may not justify it. [Codemagic](/tools/codemagic) is the closest alternative to compare.

## Pricing
Bitrise uses a freemium model priced in build credits.

:::comparison Bitrise plans (checked July 19, 2026)
| Plan | Price | What is included |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hobby | $0 | 300 build credits per month, 1 team member, and 1 private app on shared macOS and Linux machines |
| Starter | $89 per month billed annually, or $99 billed monthly | Unlimited builds on credits, up to 10 team members, and 2 private apps |
| Pro | $200 per month billed annually, or $218 billed monthly | Higher build volume, unlimited team members, and unlimited private apps |
| Enterprise | Custom | Dedicated or self-hosted infrastructure and volume terms for large teams |
:::

Bitrise meters builds in credits you spend on machine time, so a heavier machine or a longer build consumes more per run. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Bitrise supports native iOS and Android, React Native, and Flutter. You connect a repository, and it detects the project and scaffolds a workflow of steps you can reorder in a visual editor or edit as bitrise.yml; the iOS steps cover certificate and profile management, Xcode build and test, and distribution to TestFlight or the App Store. Expect a moderate first setup, usually an afternoon for a working iOS pipeline.

Two gotchas stand out. Code signing setup, whether through the certificate and profile steps or Bitrise-managed signing, is the common early hurdle. Builds consume credits, so watch machine size and caching to avoid burning the monthly allowance.

## Frequently asked questions
### How is Bitrise different from Codemagic?
Both host mobile CI/CD on macOS machines. Bitrise centers on a large library of visual, reorderable steps and fixed monthly plans priced in build credits, while [Codemagic](/tools/codemagic) leans on a YAML config with a free minute allowance and pay-as-you-go billing. A solo developer who wants a guided step editor may prefer Bitrise, while one who wants to pay only per build may prefer Codemagic.

### Can I still use fastlane on Bitrise?
Yes. Bitrise includes steps that run [fastlane](/tools/fastlane) lanes, so existing lanes for signing, screenshots, or store upload carry over. Many teams keep the release logic in fastlane and use Bitrise as the trigger and hosting layer.

### Is the free Hobby plan enough for a solo app?
For a light cadence it can be. Hobby includes 300 build credits per month for 1 app and 1 member (checked July 19, 2026). Credits are consumed by build time, so a few iOS builds per week fit, but heavy or frequent builds push you to a paid plan.

emerge-tools.pricing_summary: No public prices; a free Open Source tier plus quote-based Indie and Team plans, oriented toward larger teams and available only by contacting sales.
pros: It analyzes the built binary, so it catches app-size and startup regressions that unit tests and crash reporting do not surface.
It plugs into existing CI as an upload-and-gate step, so it can block a regression in a pull request before release.
Competitor benchmarking gives an outside reference for whether your app size is reasonable for its category.
A free Open Source tier makes the tooling available at no cost for qualifying open-source projects.
cons: Paid pricing is quote-only, so you cannot gauge cost without contacting sales.
The product is oriented toward larger, Scale-stage teams, so a solo indie app is off its core audience.
The value depends on an existing CI pipeline and a steady release cadence, so a manual, occasional release gets little from it.
It addresses size and performance, not crashes, so it does not replace a crash reporter.
emerge-tools.body_markdown:
Emerge Tools is app size and performance monitoring for mobile teams. It analyzes your build to track binary size, flag size regressions on each release, benchmark against competitors, and catch performance issues such as slow startup, with snapshot testing and build diffing included. It is aimed at teams where app size and release quality are measurable business concerns.

For most solo iOS developers this is more than the moment calls for. Size monitoring and regression gating pay off when an app is large, when downloads are sensitive to size, or when a team ships often enough that regressions slip through. A pre-revenue or small app rarely needs it, and both the product and the pricing are oriented toward larger, Scale-stage teams. If your main concern is crashes rather than size, [Sentry](/tools/sentry) is closer to what you need.

## Pricing
Emerge Tools does not publish flat prices (checked July 19, 2026). It presents tiers: an Open Source tier that is free for qualifying open-source projects, an Indie tier for solo developers with limited monthly builds and concurrency, and a Team tier with the full toolset, enforced checks, and enterprise features. The paid tiers are quote-based and direct you to contact the company for a figure, so treat cost as a sales conversation rather than a self-serve signup. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm current numbers on the official pricing page.

## Setup and integration
Emerge Tools works by analyzing your built app, so integration means uploading builds, usually from CI. It provides tooling for iOS and Android and plugs into pipelines such as [Codemagic](/tools/codemagic), [Bitrise](/tools/bitrise), or GitHub Actions, where a step uploads each build for size and performance analysis and can fail the build on a regression. Effort is low to moderate once CI exists, mostly adding an upload step and an API token.

Two things shape the value. It depends on running against real release builds over time, so a single one-off upload tells you little. The size and performance checks are most useful gating a pull request, which assumes you already have CI in place.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is Emerge Tools worth it for a solo iOS developer?
Usually not yet. Its size monitoring, regression gating, and competitor benchmarking pay off for larger apps and teams shipping frequently (checked July 19, 2026). A small or pre-revenue app gains more from shipping features than from size analytics, and the pricing is oriented toward Scale-stage teams.

### How is Emerge Tools different from Sentry?
They watch different things. [Sentry](/tools/sentry) focuses on crashes, errors, and performance traces in production, while Emerge Tools focuses on the build itself, meaning app size, size regressions, and startup performance measured before release. Many larger teams run both, but a solo developer usually needs crash reporting first.

### Does Emerge Tools publish its prices?
No. It lists an Open Source tier and paid Indie and Team tiers, but the paid figures are quote-based and require contacting the company (checked July 19, 2026). Plan for a sales conversation rather than a public price.

fastlane.body_markdown:
fastlane is an open-source automation toolkit for mobile release work. It wraps the repetitive parts of shipping an app, such as generating localized screenshots, managing certificates and profiles, updating metadata, and uploading builds to TestFlight, the App Store, and Google Play, into lanes you define once and run with a single command. For a solo iOS developer, it turns a manual release checklist into a repeatable script.

It fits almost any iOS developer who releases more than a couple of times, because the lanes run locally or inside any CI. It does not fit if you want a hosted dashboard and managed machines out of the box, because fastlane is a command-line tool you run somewhere, not a service. For that, pair it with [Codemagic](/tools/codemagic) or [Bitrise](/tools/bitrise).

## Pricing
fastlane is free and open source under the MIT license, so there is no subscription, seat, or usage fee for the tool itself (checked July 19, 2026). Your only costs are indirect: the machine or CI minutes where the lanes run, since fastlane still needs a Mac to build and sign an iOS app. Pricing last checked July 19, 2026; confirm the current license and project status on the official site.

## Setup and integration
fastlane installs through RubyGems or Homebrew and runs on macOS for iOS work. You initialize it in your project and define lanes in a Fastfile. Common building blocks include match for shared, git-stored code signing, snapshot for localized screenshots, deliver for metadata and store upload, and pilot for TestFlight. Effort is moderate up front and pays back on every later release.

Two gotchas are worth knowing. match changes how code signing works by storing certificates in a git repository, which is powerful but needs a deliberate setup and access control. Because fastlane depends on Ruby and the Xcode command-line tools, version drift between machines can break lanes, so pinning versions helps.

## Frequently asked questions
### Is fastlane really free?
Yes. fastlane is open source under the MIT license, with no paid tier for the tool (checked July 19, 2026). The only costs are the machine or CI minutes where you run it, since building and signing an iOS app still requires a Mac.

### How does fastlane compare with Codemagic?
They solve different layers. [Codemagic](/tools/codemagic) is hosted CI that provides the machines and a dashboard, while fastlane is the automation you run on those machines. Many setups use both: fastlane defines the lanes, and Codemagic or [Bitrise](/tools/bitrise) triggers and hosts them. You can also run fastlane entirely on your own Mac with no CI at all.

### Do I still need App Store Connect if I use fastlane?
Yes. fastlane automates the steps against App Store Connect, such as uploading builds, metadata, and screenshots, but you still create the app record, products, and agreements there. It removes the repetitive clicking, not the account.
